GB1-W02-A1 - Escape Side Standing Headlo.body:
## Concept

# Escape Side Standing Headlock + Rear Takedown + Technical Mount + Armbar

**Position:** Standing  
**Category:** Defense (Self-Defense)  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 2 A1  
**Confidence:** 4/5

A practical self-defense technique against a side headlock when standing. Protect the neck, use a thumb-down V-grip and hip control with a reverse lunge to take the opponent down, then use framing and drive (not raw strength) to break the grip, recover to technical mount or side control, and finish with an armbar or back take.

GB1 Week 2 emphasizes mindset shift when strikes are possible, protecting the neck immediately, and using technique/leverage over strength.

## Setup

You are standing. Opponent takes a side headlock (arm around your neck from the side), squeezing and possibly trying to take you down or strike.

- Immediately protect your neck (tuck chin, frame).
- Control their hip and wrist.

Wikilink connections:
- Directly related to [[GB1-W02-B1 - Escape Rear Headlock + Forward Takedown - Outside Hook Takedown]] (the rear headlock counterpart in the same week).
- Guard recovery and technical mount concepts connect to [[GB1-W01-A1 - Escape from Side Mount with Punches + Recovering Guard + Technical Lift]] and [[GB1-W02-A2 - Escape Side Mount Recovering Closed Guard]].
- Armbar finish links to the closed guard armbar material we have enriched.

## Execution

### Protect the Neck & Establish Control
1. As soon as the headlock is applied, tuck your chin hard and turn slightly toward the opponent to relieve pressure on your neck/jaw. Never look away/out.
2. Hug their hip with one arm (control their base) and control their wrist with the other hand. Create a strong frame.

### Reverse Lunge Takedown
1. Secure a thumb-down V-grip behind their near knee with one hand while keeping the other on their hip.
2. Step back with a strong reverse lunge, pulling their hip and driving them backward/down to the mat.
3. As they go down, spin your body around to stay on top or move to a dominant angle while they are still holding the headlock.

### Break the Grip with Frame & Drive (Key GB Detail)
1. Once on the ground (often ending in a modified mount/side position with them still holding the headlock), do **not** put both hands on the mat and try to lift your head with strength — they will beat you.
2. Instead: Block their hip with one hand/arm. Place your other hand on their shoulder or head to open space.
3. Create a strong frame/wedge: Hand over the head + hand on the inside. Keep the wrist stiff (no limp wrist).
4. Drive forward with your back/hips and the frame to break their grip on your head while collecting their bottom arm.
5. This uses leverage and their own pressure against them.

### Finish to Technical Mount or Armbar/Back Take
1. Once the grip is broken, step over to technical mount or recover side control.
2. From there, options include taking the back or finishing the exposed arm with an armbar (feed the arm, high leg placement, hip elevation — consistent with the GB-reviewed armbar mechanics from closed guard).

### Hand Placement & References
- **Neck protection**: Tuck chin + hug the hip + wrist control immediately. This is non-negotiable in GB self-defense.
- **V-grip on knee + hip control**: Thumb down behind the knee for the takedown lever; hand on hip for base control during the reverse lunge.
- **Frame instead of lift**: Hand over head + inside hand creating a stiff wedge/frame. Drive with the whole body (back muscles engaged) rather than arms only. This is the key detail from the GB Harrogate Week 2 instruction.
- **Post-bridge arm collection**: After breaking the grip, immediately control their bottom arm for the armbar or back take transition.

## Common Mistakes
- Looking away from the headlock (increases pressure and risk of being taken down awkwardly).
- Using raw strength to lift the head after being taken down (they beat you with size/strength).
- Weak or late framing (allows them to keep the headlock or strike).
- Not spinning/angling properly during the takedown (ends up in a bad position).
- Forgetting to immediately attack or improve position once the grip is broken.

## Key Principles
- **Protect the neck first, always**: Tuck chin, frame the hip/wrist. Mindset shift when strikes or headlocks are involved (GB self-defense emphasis).
- **Leverage over strength on the takedown**: Reverse lunge + V-grip on the knee uses their base against them.
- **Frame and drive to break the grip (GB Harrogate/Scottsdale mechanic)**: Create a stiff wedge and drive forward with the whole body instead of trying to bench-press your head up. This is the technique that works against larger opponents.
- **Use their reaction**: In the roll reversal variation (GB Scottsdale), shift weight to "tease" them forward, then transfer when they push back — no strength required.
- **Immediate transition to dominant position or finish**: Once the headlock is broken, move to technical mount/side control and look for the armbar or back take (links to the enriched armbar and back material).
- **Consistent hip and framing mechanics**: The hip movement and framing here align with the hip escape, posting base, and angle creation principles reviewed across our GB Scottsdale closed guard and escape content.

## Media & Visual References

**GB Affiliate Priority (per GB Greensboro rule):**
- Gracie Barra Harrogate — "Side Headlock Escape" (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FXW6LE_6wQ4). Direct GB affiliate instruction with clear Week 2 curriculum flavor (protect neck, V-grip reverse lunge, hip block, frame/drive instead of strength lift, follow-ups). Excellent match for the self-defense emphasis in GB1 Week 2.
- Gracie Barra Scottsdale — "Headlock Escape 4 Roll Reversal" (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ODF7dH8DjIg). High-quality GB Scottsdale affiliate instruction on tucking the chin, locking hands high on the back, walking the hips, bridging to shift weight, and using the opponent's reaction to reverse. Core principles transfer directly.

These are prioritized after Greensboro-specific results were limited; both are strong GB affiliate sources teaching mechanics consistent with the user's training at Gracie Barra Greensboro.

Cross-references: The headlock defense principles here pair with the mount/side punch defense cards from Week 1 and the rear headlock escape in the same week (B1).

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- Chin tucked, hip hugged, wrist controlled in the standing headlock.
- Thumb-down V-grip behind the knee + hand on hip for the reverse lunge takedown.
- Hip block + hand on shoulder opening space on the ground.
- Strong frame (hand over head + inside hand) driving forward to break the grip.
- Transition to technical mount or armbar/back take.

**Hand Placement & References**  
The neck protection (chin tuck + hip hug + wrist control), V-grip lever for the takedown, and especially the stiff frame + whole-body drive to break the grip (instead of arm strength) are the critical details from the GB Harrogate and Scottsdale instruction. These framing and leverage principles are consistent with the punch defense and hip escape work in Week 1 and the closed guard material. Drill together with the rear headlock escape (W02-B1) and the side mount recovery cards in this week.

## Personal Cues & Notes

GB1-W02-A2 - Escape Side Mount Recoverin.body:
## Concept

# Escape Side Mount Recovering Closed Guard

**Position:** Side Control  
**Category:** Defense  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 2 A2  
**Confidence:** 4/5

From bottom side control, use frames, hip escape (shrimp), and knee replacement to create space and recover closed guard. This is the foundational side control escape that leads into the more advanced variations in A3 and B2.

The hip escape, framing, and guard recovery mechanics are consistent with the GB affiliate instruction we have reviewed.

## Setup

You are in bottom side control. Opponent has good chest pressure, cross-face or underhook.

- Create immediate frames.
- Hips must be active.

Wikilink connections:
- Direct foundation for [[GB1-W02-A3 - Escape Side Mount When Opponent Blocks Hip + Head, Recover Guard Leg Over Top]] (the blocked-hip variation).
- Guard recovery links to [[GB1-W01-A2 - Pulling to Closed Guard Using Foot on Hip]] and the closed guard system.
- Similar hip escape principles to the Week 1 side/mount punch defense cards.

## Execution

### Frame Immediately
1. Post your near forearm across their neck/shoulder or hip to create a barrier and relieve chest pressure.
2. Bring your far hand to control their base arm or head if possible.

### Hip Escape (Shrimp) + Create Angle
1. Bridge slightly and shrimp your hips away from their hips, creating space and an angle.
2. Walk your shoulders/hips while keeping the frame tight.

### Replace with Knee + Recover Guard
1. As space opens, bring your near knee up between you and their hip (knee shield replacement).
2. Use the space and knee position to pull them into closed guard (foot on hip or underhook pull — see A2 from Week 1).
3. Once in guard, immediately work posture breaking.

### Hand Placement & References
- **Forearm frame**: Near forearm across their structure to jam and create the initial space.
- **Knee replacement**: The knee comes in to replace the frame as you shrimp — this is the transition point to guard.
- **Pulling mechanics**: Same foot-on-hip or underhook pull as the Week 1 guard pull card.

## Common Mistakes
- Staying flat and only pushing with arms (they settle heavier).
- Poor framing (no space created for the shrimp).
- Not replacing the frame with the knee quickly enough.
- Recovering guard without immediately breaking their posture (they pass or smash).

## Key Principles
- **Frame first**: Create a barrier before trying to move.
- **Hip escape creates the space and angle**: The shrimp + shoulder walk is the engine (same hip movement principle reviewed in GB Scottsdale closed guard and escape content).
- **Knee replacement**: The knee shield is the bridge from side control defense to closed guard recovery.
- **Immediate attack after recovery**: Once in guard, transition straight into the X-Collar or other attacks.
- **Consistent hip and framing across defenses**: The mechanics here are the same family as the punch defense escapes and the headlock work in this week.

## Media & Visual References

**GB Affiliate Priority (per GB Greensboro rule):**
- Gracie Barra Scottsdale Weekly Technique Videos playlist (https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLr-z5vDCpY3L0Z4aj3Sy0pgVXQjhnAUkW), including their Week 2 side mount escape content (high leg method variations). Provides the hip escape, framing, and guard recovery principles in GB affiliate instruction.

Cross-references: The full Week 2 side mount escape family (A3, B2) and the Week 1 guard recovery material.

## Personal Cues & Notes

GB1-W02-A3 - Escape Side Mount When Oppo.body:
## Concept

# Escape Side Mount When Opponent Blocks Hip + Head, Recover Guard Leg Over Top

**Position:** Side Control  
**Category:** Defense  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 2 A3  
**Confidence:** 4/5

When the opponent blocks your hip and head in side control (preventing the standard shrimp), use a leg-over-the-top recovery or high leg method to still get to guard or a better position. This is the "what if they block?" variation of the basic side mount escape.

The high leg and framing mechanics align with GB affiliate side mount escape instruction.

## Setup

Bottom side control. Opponent has blocked your near hip and is controlling your head (common defensive reaction to your escape attempt).

Wikilink connections:
- Direct follow-up to [[GB1-W02-A2 - Escape Side Mount Recovering Closed Guard]] (the standard version).
- Connects to the turtle escape (B2) if the leg-over recovery is stuffed.

## Execution

### Recognize the Block & Adjust
1. When they block your hip and head, do not force the standard shrimp.
2. Use frames to create space despite the block.

### High Leg / Leg Over the Top Recovery
1. Bring your far leg high (high leg method) or work to get your near leg over their blocking arm/hip.
2. Use the high leg as leverage to create the angle and recover guard (or at least half guard with good position).
3. Pull them into closed guard using the foot-on-hip or similar once space allows.

### Hand Placement & References
- Strong frames against the block on hip and head.
- High leg placement for leverage (similar "knee line" concept to armbar/triangle entries we have reviewed).
- Same pulling mechanics into guard as the other recoveries.

## Common Mistakes
- Forcing the blocked side instead of switching to the high leg variation.
- Weak frames (they maintain the block easily).
- Not using the high leg as an active lever.

## Key Principles
- **Adapt to their defense**: When they block one path, have a secondary (high leg / leg over top) ready.
- **High leg for leverage**: Creates the angle even when the standard shrimp is blocked.
- **Consistent recovery to attack**: Get to guard and immediately threaten (X-Collar, triangle, etc.).

## Media & Visual References

**GB Affiliate Priority (per GB Greensboro rule):**
- Gracie Barra Scottsdale playlist (including Week 2 side mount escape high leg method content). Provides the advanced variation mechanics in GB affiliate instruction.

Cross-references: The full Week 2 side mount family and the closed guard recovery material.

## Personal Cues & Notes

GB1-W02-B1 - Escape Rear Headlock + Forw.body:
## Concept

# Escape Rear Headlock + Forward Takedown / Outside Hook Takedown

**Position:** Standing  
**Category:** Defense (Self-Defense)  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 2 B1  
**Confidence:** 4/5

Against a rear headlock (standing), protect the neck, use a forward takedown or outside hook to bring the opponent down, and finish with control or a submission. This is the rear-headlock counterpart to the side headlock work in A1.

The neck protection and leverage principles are consistent with the GB affiliate headlock escapes we have reviewed.

## Setup

Standing. Opponent has a rear headlock (arm around your neck from behind), possibly with their other hand controlling your arm or body.

Wikilink connections:
- Direct partner to [[GB1-W02-A1 - Escape Side Standing Headlock + Rear Takedown + Technical Mount + Armbar]].
- Takedown and hook mechanics link to the turtle and back work in this week.

## Execution

### Protect the Neck
1. Tuck your chin immediately. Create frames with your hands/arms on their controlling arm.
2. Control their hip or leg if possible.

### Forward Takedown or Outside Hook
1. Use a forward drop or outside hook on their leg to take them down while protecting your neck.
2. Once down, use framing and drive (similar to the side headlock break) to improve position or finish.

### Hand Placement & References
- Chin tuck + arm frames on their controlling arm.
- Hip/leg control for the takedown or hook.
- Strong frames on the ground to break any remaining grip.

## Common Mistakes
- Not protecting the neck first (choke or neck crank risk).
- Using strength instead of leverage on the takedown.

## Key Principles
- **Neck protection is non-negotiable**: Same as the side headlock.
- **Leverage on the takedown/hook**: Use their base and your movement, not raw power.
- **Consistent framing and drive**: The break-the-grip mechanic from the GB Harrogate/Scottsdale headlock work applies here.

## Media & Visual References

**GB Affiliate Priority (per GB Greensboro rule):**
- The same GB Harrogate and Scottsdale headlock escape videos (see A1 card) for the core neck protection, framing, and leverage principles.
- GB Scottsdale playlist for additional standing defense context.

Cross-references: The side headlock card (A1) and the turtle/back material in this week.

## Personal Cues & Notes

GB1-W02-B2 - Escape Side Mount to Turtle.body:
## Concept

# Escape Side Mount to Turtle Position

**Position:** Side Control  
**Category:** Defense  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 2 B2  
**Confidence:** 4/5

When standard guard recovery is not possible or too risky in side control, escape to turtle position as a safe intermediate, then work from there (recover guard, take the back, or stand up).

This is the "bail out" option when the opponent is heavy or blocking your normal paths.

## Setup

Bottom side control. Opponent has strong pressure or is blocking your hip/head escape attempts.

Wikilink connections:
- Direct alternative to the guard recovery cards in A2/A3.
- Leads into [[GB1-W02-B3 - Double Leg Takedown from Turtle Position]] and back/turtle material.

## Execution

### Create Enough Space to Turtle
1. Use frames and a partial shrimp to create just enough space to turn into turtle (chest down, knees in).
2. Protect your neck and arms during the turn.

### From Turtle
1. Once in turtle, immediately posture up or work to recover guard, take the back if they overcommit, or stand up.
2. Common follow-ups include the double leg from turtle (B3) or back takes.

### Hand Placement & References
- Strong frames during the turn to turtle.
- Neck and arm protection while turtled.
- Hip and leg positioning for the follow-up attack or recovery.

## Common Mistakes
- Turtling too early or without protection (gives them easy attacks).
- Staying flat in turtle without immediate action.

## Key Principles
- **Turtle as a safe bailout**: Better than being smashed in side control when other escapes are blocked.
- **Immediate action from turtle**: Do not stall — recover guard, attack, or stand.
- **Consistent framing and hip movement**: The principles from the other side control escapes apply during the transition.

## Media & Visual References

**GB Affiliate Priority (per GB Greensboro rule):**
- GB Scottsdale playlist for side control to turtle transitions and the follow-up attacks from turtle.

Cross-references: The full Week 2 side control family and the turtle/back cards.

## Personal Cues & Notes

GB1-W02-B3 - Double Leg Takedown from Tu.body:
## Concept

# Double Leg Takedown from Turtle Position

**Position:** Turtle / Standing  
**Category:** Takedown  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 2 B3  
**Confidence:** 4/5

From turtle position (after escaping side control or defending a takedown), shoot a double leg takedown on the opponent as they approach or posture, using the turtle as a base to explode into the shot.

This turns a defensive position into an offensive takedown opportunity.

## Setup

You are in turtle. Opponent is circling, posturing, or reaching for you.

Wikilink connections:
- Direct follow-up from [[GB1-W02-B2 - Escape Side Mount to Turtle Position]].
- Double leg mechanics link to the standing takedown work in Week 3.

## Execution

### Set Up the Shot from Turtle
1. From turtle, posture up or create a momentary base.
2. As the opponent commits or reaches, explode into a double leg shot, driving through their legs while using your turtle base for power.

### Finish the Takedown
1. Secure the double leg, run the pipe or lift, and finish to a dominant position (side control or mount).

### Hand Placement & References
- Strong base from the turtle position for the explosive shot.
- Head position and hand control during the double leg (inside or outside depending on the finish).

## Common Mistakes
- Shooting without a good base or timing (they sprawl easily).
- Poor head position on the shot.

## Key Principles
- **Turtle as a launch point**: Not just a defensive shell — it can be used to explode into attacks.
- **Timing on the shot**: Wait for their commitment or reach.
- **Consistent double leg mechanics**: Same as the standing double legs taught elsewhere in GB1.

## Media & Visual References

**GB Affiliate Priority (per GB Greensboro rule):**
- GB Scottsdale playlist for turtle work and the double leg entry from turtle in their Week 2 curriculum content.

Cross-references: The turtle escape (B2) and the standing takedown cards in Week 3.

## Personal Cues & Notes
